Author: Dr Vineeth Veetil B
Title: From Experiment to Essential: The AI Code Generation Dilemma
Text:
AI is Writing Your Code — But Who’s Making Sure It’s Right?
Many technological revolutions start as a toy, then become a tool, and then an essential. AI code generation has followed that script. From obscurity a couple years ago to over 95% of developers admitting to use AI generated code, it has been a whirlwind journey.
However, speed without control is chaos. Organizations are discovering that if you let AI run wild in your codebase, it will introduce security risks, technical debt, debugging nightmares, and an avalanche of code that nobody understands. If you don’t control it, it will control you.
Granted, many of these problems are diminishing with state-of-the-art models. Issues like security vulnerabilities, hallucinations, and code churn are getting mitigated with each iteration. LLMs are getting better by the day. However, until we have trusted benchmarks — similar to self-driving car safety ratings to drive hands-free — the onus remains on developers to ensure AI-generated code is secure, maintainable, and up to industry standards.
The Hidden Dangers Lurking in AI Code
1. Security: The Trojan Horse Inside Your Codebase
By default, AI does not care too much about security. It requires careful alignment, prompting and/or fine-tuning to make that happen. So, for instance, AI may not consciously think of SQL injection attacks or understand security threats inherently. More than 50% of companies have reported security issues in AI-generated code and research from Stanford shows that AI-assisted developers write less secure code — while paradoxically believing it to be more secure. That’s a dangerous combination.
AI-generated vulnerabilities aren’t hypothetical — they’re happening right now. In one study, 40% of Copilot-generated code contained exploitable security flaws. Imagine hiring a developer who writes insecure code 40% of the time. You wouldn’t. Yet, we’re handing AI the keys to our software supply chain, often without adequate safeguards.
Newer AI models are improving their ability to generate more secure code, but they’re not foolproof. Without the right prompts, AI may generate bloated, inconsistent, undocumented code. It can sometimes behave like an intern — working faster than a senior engineer but refusing to leave comments, follow architecture guidelines, or prioritize maintainability.
2. Maintainability: AI’s Code Rot Problem
One large-scale analysis of 153 million lines of AI-generated code found a massive increase in copy-pasting and redundancy. AI doesn’t refactor unless explicitly instructed. It simply generates more code, which can quickly spiral into an unmanageable mess.
That said, cutting-edge AI models are improving at writing cleaner, more idiomatic code. Features like real-time linting, better training data, and reinforcement learning are reducing sloppy AI output. But again, until we have a universal benchmark for AI-generated code quality, developers need to verify, refactor, and document AI-assisted contributions manually.
3. Code Churn: Writing More, Reverting More
Developers using AI code assistants tend to write more code, but they also tend to rewrite that code a lot. One study found that code churn — how often recently written code gets modified or deleted — has doubled in the AI era. More than 7% of AI-generated code changes are reverted within two weeks.
However, as models become more aligned with best practices, we’re seeing early signs of churn rates stabilizing. Even so, developers must still validate AI-generated code against real-world constraints and business logic.
4. Debugging: The Black Box Problem
AI-generated code often feels like a black box. It might work, but when it breaks, you have no idea why. In enterprise settings, this has already led to production outages — teams deploying AI-generated code without fully understanding it, only to have it fail spectacularly in the real world . Debugging AI code isn’t just hard — it’s often harder than writing the code from scratch in the first place. And when developers don’t fully understand their own code, that’s a problem that scales very badly.
Conclusion: AI is a Force Multiplier — If You Stay in Control
In the future, AI may become autonomous, but as of now, just like self-driving cars, AI coding tools are not a replacement for developers. They are multipliers— and multipliers can amplify both good and bad practices. Used correctly, AI can accelerate development, reduce boilerplate, and increase productivity. Used recklessly, it can generate security holes, technical debt, and debugging nightmares at scale.
The good news is that AI models are improving fast. Many of the issues plaguing AI-generated code today will gradually disappear— just as self-driving cars have become safer with better data, real-world testing, and stricter regulations. But until AI-generated code is benchmarked with the same rigor as self-driving vehicles, the responsibility is on developers to ensure quality, security, and maintainability.
The best engineers will not be the ones who let AI write all their code. They’ll be the ones who learn how to control AI, enforce quality, and own the results. AI can make you a 10x engineer — but only if you’re in the driver’s seat.
